content.subtitle: Uses
items: Telling jokes and other informal stories

content.subtitle: Examples
items: She never **checks** her email before noon.
My uncle **runs** a small bakery in the town centre.
So, a woman **walks** into a bookshop and asks for a book on confidence ...
Torres **shoots**! And it's in! What a goal!
The documentary **explores** the hidden world of deep-sea creatures.
You **take** the second turning on the left and the station is right in front of you.
The summit **begins** on the 15th of next month.
I'll let you know as soon as I **hear** back from them.

content.subtitle: Emphatic Present Simple
content.description: To emphasise contrast or strong feeling.
examples: She may not enjoy cooking, but she **does make** an excellent pasta.
I **do wish** you'd stop interrupting me when I'm talking!

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Temporary situations and series of actions
Annoying or amusing habits (usually with always)
Background information in jokes and other informal stories

content.subtitle: Examples
items: The chef **is preparing** the sauce for tonight's special dish.
We **are studying** Victorian literature in class this term.
I **am not attending** the gym while the renovations are taking place.
Fewer and fewer people **are relying** on printed maps to navigate.
My flatmate **is always leaving** the bathroom in a mess!
A woman walks into a bookshop. She **is carrying** a long list of titles she wants ...
**Are you presenting** your research at the conference next week?
I'll probably feel more relaxed once I **am sitting** on the plane.

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Situations and states that started in the past and are still true
A series of actions continuing up to now
Completed actions at a time in the past which is not important or relevant
Completed actions where the important thing is the present result

content.subtitle: Examples
items: She**'s worked** as a marine biologist for over a decade.
He**'s completed** three marathons, two triathlons and a charity swim this year.
**Have** you ever **visited** a country where you didn't speak the language?
The government**'s announced** a new policy on renewable energy.
I've just heard the most incredible piece of news.
Give me a call once you**'ve landed** at the airport.

content.subtitle: Note on American English
content.description: In American English, the past simple is often used instead of the present perfect simple.
examples: US: **Did** you **finish** the report yet? UK: **Have** you **finished** the report yet?
US: I already **sent** the email. UK: I**'ve** already **sent** the email.

examples.note: In informal American English, gotten is sometimes used as a past participle instead of got when it means 'obtain', 'become' or 'move'.

examples: US: I **haven't gotten** the package yet. UK: I **haven't got** the package yet.

content.subtitle: Uses
items: Actions and situations continuing up to the present (or just before the present)

content.subtitle: Examples
items: We've **all been debating** where to hold the end-of-year party and still can't agree.
I won't apply for the promotion until I've **been working** here for at least eighteen months.

content.subtitle: Be careful
content.description: We usually use the present perfect simple to specify a particular number of times/things. We usually use the present perfect continuous to emphasise the duration of an action/situation.
examples: ✓ I've **sent** three follow-up emails this morning.
✓ I've **worked here** for three years. (no emphasis)
✓ I've **been working** here for three years. (emphasises the duration)

sections.title: Stative and Non-Stative Uses of Verbs

content.subtitle: Stative Verbs (generally used in simple tenses)
Be careful
content.description: Many of these verbs can be used in continuous tenses when they describe actions rather than states. Examples: appeal, be, consider, depend, feel, have, include, look, mean, mind, see, smell, taste, think, weigh.
examples: ✓ I **think** it's essential to back up your files regularly. (state: think = believe)
✓ I**'m thinking** of taking a gap year before university. (action: think = consider)
